instruction.global: Pour a batch of strawberries into the glass blender jar until they settle at the bottom.
steps: Drop the first few strawberries into the blender, showing motion blur as they fall.
Increase the flow of strawberries, filling the air above the blender with many falling fruits.
Clear the air as the strawberries finish falling and settle at the bottom of the blender.

explain: Gravity pulls the initial strawberries downward, creating vertical motion blur as they accelerate into the jar.
A larger volume of strawberries is released, creating a dense cluster of falling objects following a gravitational trajectory.
The strawberries complete their fall and come to rest at the base of the jar, leaving the space above clear.
invariants: Background kitchen scene including the fruit bowl and counter
Position and transparency of the glass blender jar
Consistent lighting and shallow depth of field

instruction.global: Drop the coin from its elevated position onto the grey surface until it comes to a complete rest.
steps: Move the coin down to the surface and tilt it slightly as it makes initial contact.
Lower the tilted edge of the coin closer to the surface as it begins to settle.
Place the coin completely flat on the surface in its final resting position.

explain: Gravity pulls the coin downward, and upon hitting the surface, it begins to wobble and tilt due to the impact force.
Kinetic energy is dissipated through friction and small vibrations, causing the coin's tilt angle to decrease as it loses momentum.
The coin reaches a state of static equilibrium where it lies perfectly flat on the horizontal plane.

instruction.global: Drop three coins into the empty glass jar until they settle at the bottom.
steps: Show coins falling through the air towards the open mouth of the glass jar.
Render three coins inside the jar, with some tilted or mid-bounce after hitting the bottom.
Position the three coins so they are lying flat and stationary at the base of the jar.

explain: Gravity pulls the coins downward from an external source into the jar's opening.
The coins collide with the bottom of the jar, causing them to bounce and tumble within the glass walls.
Kinetic energy is lost through successive impacts, causing the coins to come to a rest in a stable horizontal orientation.
invariants: Consistent studio lighting and reflections on the glass

instruction.global: Show a glass vial falling, impacting the floor, and shattering into scattered shards.
steps: Depict the glass vial hitting the floor and fracturing into multiple sharp pieces at the point of impact.
Scatter the glass shards outward across the floor surface, showing motion and dispersion.
Settle the glass shards into their final resting positions on the flat surface.

explain: The vial strikes the hard surface, and the impact force exceeds the structural integrity of the glass, causing it to shatter.
Kinetic energy from the collision is transferred to the resulting fragments, propelling them away from the center of impact.
Friction between the glass shards and the floor, along with gravity, brings the fragments to a complete stop.
invariants: Consistent beige background and floor color
Uniform lighting and reflections on the glass
Material properties of the transparent glass

instruction.global: Fill the empty glass jar by dropping several coins into it, with some coins landing on the surface outside.
steps: Show multiple coins falling from above into the open mouth of the glass jar with motion blur.
Depict the coins hitting the bottom of the jar, with some bouncing and two coins flying out to the left and right sides.
Let all coins come to a complete rest at the bottom of the jar and on the flat surface outside.

explain: Gravity pulls the coins downward into the jar, creating motion blur as they accelerate.
Kinetic energy from the fall causes the coins to collide with the glass and each other, resulting in some coins being deflected out of the jar.
Friction and gravity cause the coins to lose momentum and settle into stable, resting positions.
invariants: Glass jar position, shape, and material transparency
Neutral grey background and lighting conditions

instruction.global: Show a single water droplet forming at the faucet tip, elongating under gravity, and then detaching to fall.
steps: Increase the volume of the water droplet hanging from the faucet aerator until it becomes a full sphere.
Elongate the water droplet downward, creating a narrow neck as it prepares to detach.
Remove the main droplet as it falls out of frame, leaving only a small residual amount of water on the aerator mesh.

explain: Surface tension holds the accumulating water to the faucet, forming a growing hemispherical drop as more water leaks out.
As the mass of the droplet increases, the force of gravity begins to overcome surface tension, causing the drop to stretch and form a thin neck.
The neck of the droplet breaks at the point of instability, allowing the main mass to fall while the remaining water retracts back to the faucet tip.
invariants: Blurred background tiles and circular logo.
Position and metallic texture of the faucet and aerator.
Dim, warm lighting and consistent depth of field.

instruction.global: Show a sequence of pecan halves falling, bouncing, and scattering across a wooden table surface.
steps: Increase the number of pecans falling from the top and show them descending toward the wooden surface.
Depict the pecans making contact with the table, with some bouncing upward and others beginning to roll.
Show the pecans settling across the table surface as more pieces continue to fall into the frame from above.

explain: Gravity accelerates the pecans downward, and additional pieces enter the frame from the top.
The pecans undergo elastic collisions with the hard wooden surface, causing them to bounce and rotate based on their impact angle.
Kinetic energy is lost through friction and repeated impacts, causing earlier pecans to settle while new ones maintain high velocity.
invariants: Static camera viewpoint at a low angle.
Wooden table texture and warm lighting.
Consistent appearance and scale of the pecan halves.

instruction.global: Drop a film strip from the top right so that it falls past the white pillow and lands on the bed surface.
steps: Introduce a film strip entering from the top right corner with significant motion blur.
Move the film strip further down the frame, positioning it vertically in front of the pillow.
Place the film strip flat on the bed surface in front of the pillow, removing the motion blur as it comes to rest.

explain: The film strip enters the frame from above, accelerated by gravity, resulting in motion blur.
The strip continues its downward trajectory, passing through the mid-air space in front of the pillow.
The film strip completes its fall, landing on the soft bedsheet where it loses its kinetic energy and stops.
invariants: Position and shape of the white pillow
Texture and color of the quilted bedspread

instruction.global: Simulate a pile of Australian banknotes falling and accumulating on a white surface, starting from an empty scene.
steps: Place two yellow fifty-dollar Australian banknotes on the white surface, showing slight crumpling as they land.
Add a large, messy pile of mixed yellow fifty-dollar and orange twenty-dollar Australian banknotes on top of the initial notes.
Adjust the banknotes in the pile to show them settling into a final, stable resting position with minor shifts in their overlap.

explain: Gravity pulls the first two banknotes down until they make contact with the flat surface.
A large volume of currency is dropped, causing the notes to collide, overlap, and stack into a disorganized pile.
The banknotes settle under their own weight and friction, reaching a state of physical equilibrium.

instruction.global: Introduce a new toilet paper roll falling from above to land in the foreground while the leftmost roll tips forward to face the camera.
steps: Drop a new toilet paper roll from the top center and tip the leftmost roll forward so its hollow core faces the camera.
Bring the falling roll into contact with the surface, showing it beginning to settle in front of the middle roll.
Stabilize the new roll in its final upright position and remove all motion blur.

explain: Gravity pulls a new roll downward while the leftmost roll is tipped over by an external force, changing its orientation from vertical to horizontal.
The falling roll impacts the reflective surface, losing kinetic energy as it prepares to settle.
Friction and gravity bring the newly added roll to a complete stop in a stable equilibrium on the flat surface.
invariants: Position and orientation of the two background rolls on the right

instruction.global: Simulate a sequence of gummy candies being dropped onto a flat surface until they settle into a pile.
steps: Replace the single bouncing orange gummy with two dark red gummies falling through the air, casting shadows on the white surface.
Introduce more multi-colored gummy candies hitting the surface, showing some bouncing and others sliding, creating a scattered arrangement.
Bring all the candies to a rest, forming a dense pile on the surface with clear contact shadows and no motion blur.

explain: Gravity pulls the newly introduced candies downward towards the surface.
Kinetic energy from the fall causes the candies to bounce and scatter upon impact with the surface.
Friction and loss of energy through multiple bounces cause the candies to come to a complete rest.
invariants: dark background with subtle grid texture

instruction.global: Show a continuous stream of white flakes falling and accumulating into a pile on the flat surface.
steps: Add many more flakes falling through the air and hitting the surface, creating a scattered layer with motion blur.
Increase the density of the flakes on the surface to form a central pile while more flakes continue to fall from above.
Settle the falling flakes onto the pile, making it larger and more spread out, with only a single flake remaining in mid-air.

explain: Gravity pulls the light flakes downward, causing them to collide with the surface and scatter across the floor.
Continuous deposition of material leads to the formation of a heap as flakes stack on top of each other.
The rate of falling flakes decreases, allowing the pile to stabilize and expand horizontally as the material settles.

instruction.global: Show a man shooting a basketball into an outdoor hoop, following the ball's trajectory until it passes through the net.
steps: Launch the basketball into the air towards the hoop, tilting the camera upward to follow its flight.
Continue the ball's arc until it reaches the center of the basketball rim.
Let the ball fall through the net and out of the frame, showing the empty hoop.

explain: The player exerts upward and forward force on the ball, initiating projectile motion against gravity.
The ball follows a parabolic trajectory, reaching its peak height as it approaches the hoop's coordinates.
Gravity pulls the ball downward through the rim, and the net flexes as the ball passes through it.
invariants: Transparent backboard and metal rim design

instruction.global: Show a shower of US dollar bills falling through the frame from top to bottom against a black background.
steps: Move the initial cluster of bills downward and introduce more bills from the top, allowing them to begin spreading out.
Continue the downward motion of all bills, increasing the density in the center of the frame as they tumble and rotate.
Clear the majority of the bills from the frame as they fall past the bottom edge, leaving only a few trailing bills still in view.

explain: Gravity pulls the bills downward while air resistance causes them to flutter and separate from the initial cluster.
The bills continue to accelerate downwards, tumbling and rotating randomly due to aerodynamic forces acting on the thin paper.
Most bills exit the frame at the bottom as their downward trajectory continues, with only the last few bills in the sequence remaining visible.
invariants: Consistent studio lighting on the bills.
Appearance and denominations of the US currency.

instruction.global: Drop a handful of small toy carrots onto the blue surface until they settle in a scattered pile.
steps: Introduce the first few toy carrots onto the blue surface.
Drop a larger quantity of toy carrots, showing some in mid-air and others bouncing off the surface.
Allow all the falling carrots to come to rest, forming a scattered arrangement on the blue background.

explain: Gravity pulls the initial carrots toward the surface, where they make first contact.
A mass of carrots falls simultaneously, creating multiple collisions with the floor and each other, resulting in chaotic bouncing and tumbling.
Kinetic energy dissipates through friction and impact, leaving the carrots in a stable, static distribution across the frame.
invariants: Solid cyan-blue background color and texture.
Visual design of the toy carrots (orange conical body with green leafy tops).

instruction.global: Add falling and settled feathers around the egg and nest on the dark surface.
steps: Place two light-colored feathers on the surface to the left of the nest.
Introduce more feathers, including one falling from the top right with motion blur and others landing on the right side of the nest.
Settle the falling feathers and add more fluffy feathers around the base and right side of the nest.

explain: Feathers land on the flat surface to the left of the nest due to gravity.
Additional feathers are dropped into the scene; some are captured mid-air while others have already landed on the right.
The airborne feathers settle onto the surface and the nest, increasing the total volume of feathers accumulated.
invariants: Dark blue background color and texture
Position and appearance of the egg
Position and structure of the nest

instruction.global: Show a handful of silver paperclips falling from above, bouncing on the white surface, and coming to a complete rest scattered across the table.
steps: Introduce a shower of silver paperclips falling through the air with motion blur, with several already impacting and bouncing off the white surface.
Reduce the number of paperclips in the air as they settle onto the surface, showing only a few still in mid-bounce or falling from a higher point.
Bring all paperclips to a complete stop, lying flat and scattered across the white surface in a static state.

explain: Gravity accelerates the paperclips downward, and their collision with the hard surface converts kinetic energy into bounces and lateral movement.
Energy is dissipated through successive inelastic collisions with the table and air resistance, causing the paperclips to lose height and velocity.
Friction and gravity overcome the remaining kinetic energy, bringing the paperclips into a state of static equilibrium on the flat surface.
invariants: Material properties of the metal paperclips

instruction.global: Drop a handful of silver paperclips onto a white surface against a black background until they come to a complete rest.
steps: Introduce silver paperclips falling from the top of the frame, with several impacting the white surface and exhibiting motion blur.
Show additional paperclips falling while those already on the surface bounce, tumble, and scatter across the table.
Render all paperclips lying flat and stationary on the white surface, removing all motion blur.

explain: Gravity pulls the paperclips downward, creating motion blur as they accelerate toward the surface.
The paperclips collide with the hard surface, causing them to bounce and rotate as kinetic energy is partially conserved.
Friction and energy loss through successive collisions cause the paperclips to lose all kinetic energy and settle into stable positions.

instruction.global: Show the blue ball falling from the top left and rolling along the floor towards the left edge of the frame.
steps: Move the blue ball vertically downward to a mid-air position while maintaining its horizontal alignment.
Place the blue ball on the ground surface directly below its previous position.
Roll the blue ball further to the left along the floor, away from the central stack.

explain: Gravity causes the blue ball to accelerate downwards from its elevated starting position.
The ball completes its freefall as it impacts the solid floor surface.
The ball's remaining kinetic energy causes it to roll horizontally across the floor after impact.
invariants: Position and balance of the yellow ball on the cubes
The arrangement and appearance of the white and tan cubes
Consistent lighting and shadows from the central stack

instruction.global: Introduce several small white balls that fall into the scene, bounce on the surface, and roll away while the central balanced stack remains undisturbed.
steps: Add multiple small white balls falling from the upper left and bouncing on the dark surface around the central cubes.
Position the white balls as they settle and roll across the floor, losing their vertical bounce height.
Remove the white balls as they roll completely out of the frame to the left and right.

explain: Gravity accelerates the small balls downward, and they bounce upon impact with the floor due to elastic collision forces.
Kinetic energy is dissipated through friction and air resistance, transitioning the motion from bouncing to rolling.
The balls maintain their horizontal momentum until they exit the camera's field of view.
invariants: Position and balance of the yellow ball on the tan cube.
Alignment of the tan cube on the white cube.
Consistent directional lighting and shadows on the central objects.
Dark grey background and black floor texture.

instruction.global: Show a basketball being shot through the hoop, passing through the net, and falling out of frame.
steps: Place a basketball entering the rim from above, partially obscured by the front of the hoop.
Move the basketball further down into the net, causing the mesh to stretch and bulge outward around the ball's circumference.
Remove the basketball from the scene and restore the net to its original, relaxed hanging position.

explain: The basketball enters the hoop due to its downward trajectory and gravity.
As the ball passes through the narrow net, its physical volume forces the flexible mesh to expand and deform.
Gravity pulls the ball completely through the bottom of the net, allowing the net to return to its equilibrium state.
invariants: Weathered texture and paint chips on the backboard
Background trees and clear sky lighting
Position and structure of the metal rim and support pole

instruction.global: Show a basketball being shot into the hoop, passing through the net, and falling toward the ground.
steps: Add a basketball in the upper right quadrant of the frame, positioned as if it is on a downward trajectory toward the rim.
Move the basketball so it is centered within the orange rim, partially obscured by the white net.
Position the basketball directly below the net as it continues its vertical descent.

explain: A basketball enters the frame following a parabolic arc toward the hoop due to projectile motion.
The ball passes through the rim, interacting with the net as gravity pulls it downward.
The ball exits the bottom of the net and continues to fall freely toward the ground.
invariants: Position and appearance of the backboard and orange rim
Metal support structure and fence on the left

instruction.global: Animate the tomato falling downwards and to the left while rotating, eventually exiting the bottom of the frame.
steps: Shift the tomato slightly down and to the left while rotating it clockwise so the stem scar points towards the right.
Accelerate the tomato's descent towards the bottom-left corner, making it partially cross the lower frame boundary.
Continue the downward trajectory until only the top curve of the tomato is visible at the very bottom edge of the frame.

explain: Gravity initiates the downward motion while an initial lateral force or air resistance causes a slight rotation and leftward drift.
The object accelerates due to gravity, increasing its displacement per frame as it moves towards the edge of the field of view.
The tomato maintains its momentum and trajectory, exiting the camera's view through the bottom-left corner.
invariants: Directional lighting from the upper left

instruction.global: Simulate a shower of bullets and casings falling onto a flat surface, bouncing, and eventually settling or rolling away.
steps: Increase the number of falling bullets and casings, showing some impacting the surface and bouncing upwards.
Fill the frame with a dense cascade of falling ammunition, with multiple objects colliding with the surface and each other at various angles.
Clear the air of falling objects, leaving only a few scattered bullets and casings resting or rolling on the surface.

explain: Gravity pulls more objects downward; kinetic energy from the impact with the hard surface causes the initial projectiles to bounce.
A continuous stream of projectiles creates a high-density shower where collisions occur between falling objects and those rebounding from the surface.
The supply of falling objects ceases, and gravity combined with friction brings the remaining objects to rest on the surface while others bounce out of the frame.
invariants: Consistent studio lighting and metallic reflections
Object types limited to brass casings and copper-tipped bullets
